6 min read
Just now

--

There are great, beautiful ships. Enormous, gleaming, certain of their course. You can’t just climb aboard one — first you have to sail alongside for a while, prove yourself, learn the rules, earn the right to step on deck.
I watched those ships for a long time.
And then I understood something simple.
I don’t need to be on board.
Because one day I stopped looking at the ships themselves and looked at where they were sailing.
And I realized: I don’t want to go there.
They’re sailing toward a world where everyone agreed long ago that two plus two is roughly four. Sometimes four. Sometimes a little more. Sometimes a little less. The difference is small — they decided to call it acceptable.
Sounds absurd?
It’s actually a fairly accurate description of the foundation on which a significant part of the modern computing industry is built.
But before I explain what I mean, I should probably introduce myself.
I’m not a programmer.
I don’t have a degree in Computer Science. I’ve never written code for money. I actually set out to write a book.
But the deeper I tried to understand how modern computing systems work, the more often I ran into the same thought.
We’ve lived for decades alongside fundamental compromises that we’ve long since stopped noticing. They’re so familiar that they feel like laws of nature.
Even though they aren’t.
I never intended to become an engineer.
But once you’ve seen a crack in the foundation, it becomes very hard to pretend it isn’t there.
Over the past year I built eleven autonomous, mathematically rigorous systems. Each one came out of a single simple question:
“Why do we assume it can’t be done any other way?”
Here’s what came of it.

The Numbers You Trust Aren’t Reproducible
The IEEE 754 standard — the one underpinning financial calculations, scientific modeling, analytics, and an enormous share of modern software — does not guarantee the same result across different processors.
The same algorithm, run on Intel and on ARM, can return different low-order bits.
The industry knows this.
And considers it acceptable noise.
Let me translate that into human terms.
Imagine parachute manufacturers officially announced:
“In most cases, everything deploys correctly. The remaining cases fall within statistical margin of error.”
How many people would still want to jump?
And yet that exact posture is treated as perfectly normal in places where computational decisions get made.
I removed floating point from the places where determinism is required.
In its place — fixed-point integer arithmetic.
The same input now produces exactly the same result — bit for bit — regardless of processor, architecture, or platform.
Not “almost always.”
Always.

Why Do We Accept That Data Must Be Unpacked First?
There’s an architectural habit almost no one notices anymore.
We carefully compress enormous volumes of information.
And then, to read a single small field, we unfold the entire array back out.
Why?
Who decided it had to work that way?
It’s like having to reopen an entire archive warehouse every time you need one book — unpacking thousands of boxes, finding the right page, then packing everything back up again.
The work is being done not for the information, but for the sake of the process itself.
I tried asking a different question:
What if we don’t unpack anything at all?
The result: the system reads the needed field directly from the compressed representation.
On real Ethereum data, a single query reads around twenty thousand bytes where existing solutions are forced to read on the order of nine million.
Not because it decompresses faster.
Because decompression is no longer needed at all.

The Memory of AI Knows How to Forget What’s Inconvenient
Today, most AI memory is built around RAG.
Text is split into chunks.
Chunks become vectors.
Then the most similar one is retrieved.
It’s convenient.
But there’s a problem.
When a new fact appears that contradicts an old one, this kind of memory rarely knows how to hold both at once.
It tries to pick one.
Or to average them.
You end up with a strange situation.
Ask it:
“Is the patient alive or dead?”
and the system answers something like:
“On average — unconscious.”
That struck me as wrong.
So memory became a journal.
The past can’t be rewritten.
You can only append.
If a contradiction arises, it doesn’t disappear.
It becomes a first-class object that demands explanation.
Memory stops hiding conflict.
It starts remembering it.

We Read a Person Through an Average Person
The human reference genome isn’t the genome of any actual person.
It’s an averaged construct, assembled from a handful of donors.
And it’s against this construct that most genomes are analyzed today.
Which creates a strange situation.
The further a person’s ancestry sits from the original group, the more of their individual traits end up hidden.
Treating everyone against an averaged genome is almost like manufacturing shoes in one size only.
Statistically, everything looks fine.
In practice, it fits far from everyone.
So I abandoned that idea.
A full pass over a human genome, with functional annotation, takes a little over twenty minutes on an ordinary laptop.
The starting point is no longer an abstract “average human.”
It’s a specific human.

Drug Discovery Stops Being a Computational Problem
The same mathematical model turned out to apply to molecules as well.
Screening thousands of approved compounds takes single-digit milliseconds.
The system doesn’t just compare structures quickly.
It independently surfaces known pharmacological relationships without having them in its knowledge base.
It measures structure.
It doesn’t recall a stored answer.
When a new pathogen appears, the computational side of searching for candidate compounds stops being the bottleneck.
After that, the biologists’ work begins.

And That’s Far From All
Over the past year, six more independent systems emerged.
A code analyzer that remembers a project’s history and can see slow architectural drift.
A security system in which it’s impossible to obtain a final verdict without a complete cryptographic trail.
A monitoring system that reacts not to noise, but to a sustained trend.
An integrity proof of constant size, regardless of the volume of data.
A deterministic digital signature.
And a data-provenance system that stays verifiable even after fragmentation.
Each of them deserves its own conversation.
And each of those conversations will come.

Why I Trust My Own Results
The short answer:
I don’t.
I don’t trust my own code.
I don’t trust anyone else’s code.
And I trust code written by an AI least of all.
Most engineers write tests to prove their system works.
I write tests to prove that it lies.
Corrupted data must lead to an error.
Exceeding a memory limit must kill the process.
A thousand identical runs must produce a thousand identical results.
If my algorithm loses to an existing solution, the build doesn’t pass.
A system earns the right to exist only after it survives everything I could devise against it.

Now the Uncomfortable Part
When I started showing results, I kept hearing the same question.
“Why doesn’t this look anything like what already exists?”
It always struck me that this question is considered reasonable at all.
A new idea is, almost by definition, under no obligation to resemble an old one.
The history of science is full of cases where unfamiliarity itself became the reason for rejection.
It happened to Ignaz Semmelweis.
It has happened dozens of times since.
We call it the Semmelweis reflex.
And yet we still haven’t learned to notice it in ourselves.
I fully allow that my systems may contain errors.
Which is exactly why I’m asking you not to believe me.
Verify.
Run it.
Compare.
Break it.
Try to catch me in a mistake.
Every claim I’ve made is checkable by experiment.
Not by trust.
If a system genuinely works — that will become visible.
If it doesn’t — all the more so.

I don’t need a ticket onto those ships.
I’m not disputing how large, beautiful, or successful they are.
I simply looked, one day, at where they were headed.
And realized I want to go the other way.
Toward a place where computation is reproducible.
Where memory doesn’t rewrite its own past.
Where approximation stops being treated as the norm.
Where an engineering argument ends in an experiment, not in authority.
If you’d rather verify than believe — stay.
This is only the beginning.
Eleven detailed breakdowns are coming.
Each one will be just as checkable.
